attributes.stem:
A 68-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with three weeks of progressive confusion and lethargy. He has lost 8 kg over the past two months and reports a dry cough. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. He has a 45 pack-year smoking history. He is retired from work as a builder.

On examination, he appears cachectic. His pulse rate is 88/min, blood pressure 128/78 mmHg, respiratory rate 20/min, SpO2 94% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. He is oriented to person only. Chest examination reveals reduced air entry and dullness to percussion in the right upper zone. Investigations reveal:

Sodium: 118 mmol/L (135-145)
Potassium: 4.2 mmol/L (3.5-5.0)
Urea: 3.2 mmol/L (3.0-8.0)
Creatinine: 65 µmol/L (60-110)
Serum osmolality: 245 mOsm/kg (285-295)
Urine osmolality: 680 mOsm/kg (300-900)
Urine sodium: 85 mmol/L

A chest X-ray shows a right upper lobe mass.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features highly suggestive of small cell lung cancer (SCLC) with syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion (SIADH). The combination of significant smoking history, rapid weight loss, and SIADH strongly points towards SCLC as the underlying diagnosis.

Small cell lung cancer is strongly associated with smoking and typically presents with extensive disease and paraneoplastic syndromes. SIADH is the most common paraneoplastic syndrome in SCLC, occurring in up to 15% of cases. The laboratory findings are classic for SIADH: low serum sodium and osmolality with inappropriately concentrated urine and elevated urinary sodium.

Adenocarcinoma is the most common type of lung cancer overall and can present with paraneoplastic syndromes, but SIADH is less common. It is more typically associated with hypertrophic pulmonary osteoarthropathy.

Squamous cell carcinoma typically presents as a central mass with cavitation and is more commonly associated with hypercalcaemia due to parathyroid hormone-related protein production rather than SIADH.

Large cell carcinoma is a diagnosis of exclusion and represents about 10% of lung cancers. It is not typically associated with paraneoplastic syndromes.

Carcinoid tumour is a rare neuroendocrine tumour that typically presents with carcinoid syndrome (flushing, diarrhoea) rather than SIADH. It is also not strongly associated with smoking.

The rapid clinical course, significant weight loss, and presence of SIADH are particularly characteristic of SCLC, which tends to be more aggressive than other types of lung cancer. Early recognition is important as SCLC is initially quite chemosensitive, although prognosis remains poor.
learningPoints: Small cell lung cancer typically presents with extensive disease and paraneoplastic syndromes.
Syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion (SIADH) is the most common paraneoplastic syndrome in small cell lung cancer.
Hyponatraemia in a smoker with weight loss should raise suspicion for underlying malignancy.
references: Rudin CM, Brambilla E, Faivre-Finn C, Sage J. Small-cell lung cancer. Nat Rev Dis Primers. 2021 Jan 14;7(1):1–20.
